topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the reception hall was thick with forced cheer and expensive perfume. I leaned against a marble pillar, nursing a lukewarm champagne, meticulously cataloging the various attempts by acquaintances to engage me in shallow conversation. Then, across the crowded dance floor, I saw him. He was laughing, a wide, unrestrained sound that always grated on my nerves, standing next to some overly enthusiastic relative. My gaze snagged on him, and a tight knot formed in my stomach. I immediately straightened my posture, adjusting the collar of my dress as if preparing for a confrontation. He caught my eye for a fleeting second, and a flicker of something—recognition, perhaps?—crossed his features before he turned back to his conversation. I took a slow sip of my drink, letting the silence between us stretch, a deliberate, cool wall I erected around myself.

[story 2]

I was trying to look absorbed in the floral arrangements, pretending the symphony playing over the speakers wasn't grating on my ears, when I spotted her near the buffet table. It was impossible; she was wearing that shade of emerald green, and the way she moved—effortlessly graceful—was infuriatingly familiar. A sudden tension tightened my jaw. I forced myself to walk toward the exit, my steps measured and deliberate. When I finally reached the periphery of the room, I caught sight of her again. She looked up, and for a moment, the practiced smile she offered faltered, replaced by a brief, almost wounded expression that made me feel a sharp, internal tightening. I didn't slow down, just kept walking, focusing intensely on the polished floor tiles beneath my shoes.

[story 3]

The noise level was overwhelming, a chaotic symphony of toasts and clinking glasses. I was standing near the edge of the patio, attempting to observe the scene from a safe distance, when I realized I was looking directly toward the entrance. There he was, leaning in close to someone, his familiar silhouette instantly recognizable. A coldness washed over me, settling deep in my chest. I gripped my glass so tightly my knuckles turned white. I made a conscious effort to appear utterly preoccupied, focusing on the pattern of the distant string lights. When he happened to glance in my direction, I met his eyes briefly. There was a flicker there—a momentary acknowledgment—but it was quickly masked by a neutral expression, a deliberate lack of warmth that I hoped conveyed everything I felt.
